---
title: "Week 2: Summarize data"
author: "Andrew Heiss"
date: "2026-02-03"
---

## ggplot resources

- [Visualize data with ggplot2](https://aysps.github.io/2024-aysps-dataviz/session_1/slides/04_visualize-data.html) slides

## dplyr resources

- [Transform data with dplyr](https://aysps.github.io/2024-aysps-dataviz/session_1/slides/05_transform-data.html) slides
- [Visualizing {dplyr}’s mutate(), summarize(), group_by(), and ungroup() with animations](https://www.andrewheiss.com/blog/2024/04/04/group_by-summarize-ungroup-animations/)

## Recreation start

You can do this in a couple different ways. Here's the most compact version, with the full `gfr_slope` calculation happening in one step:

```{.r}
mdrd %>% 
  group_by(ptid) %>% 
  arrange(months) %>% 
  summarize(gfr_slope = (last(gfr) - first(gfr)) / (last(months) - first(months)))

#> # A tibble: 255 × 2
#>     ptid gfr_slope
#>    <dbl>     <dbl>
#>  1     1    -0.392
#>  2     2     0.198
#>  3     3    -0.316
#>  4     4    -0.494
#>  5     5    -0.237
#>  6     6    -0.446
#>  7     7    -1.21 
#>  8     8    -0.347
#>  9     9    -0.147
#> 10    10    -2.65 
#> # ℹ 245 more rows
#> # ℹ Use `print(n = ...)` to see more rows
```

Or you can add some intermediate steps to see what's going on behind the scenes:

```{.r}
mdrd %>% 
  group_by(ptid) %>% 
  arrange(months) %>% 
  summarize(
    gfr_diff = last(gfr) - first(gfr),
    time_diff = last(months) - first(months)
  ) %>% 
  mutate(gfr_slope = gfr_diff / time_diff)
#> # A tibble: 255 × 4
#>     ptid gfr_diff time_diff gfr_slope
#>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
#>  1     1   -14.1      35.8     -0.392
#>  2     2     7.89     39.8      0.198
#>  3     3   -12.0      38.0     -0.316
#>  4     4    -9.79     19.8     -0.494
#>  5     5    -9.63     40.6     -0.237
#>  6     6   -18.7      41.9     -0.446
#>  7     7    -9.84      8.11    -1.21 
#>  8     8   -11.1      32       -0.347
#>  9     9    -5.42     36.9     -0.147
#> 10    10   -12.9       4.86    -2.65 
#> # ℹ 245 more rows
#> # ℹ Use `print(n = ...)` to see more rows
```
